orig_args.new_str:
# Strateturn - Implementation Plan

## Overview

This document outlines the detailed implementation strategy for Strateturn, using the EARS (Easy Approach to Requirements Syntax) notation for business logic requirements. The implementation follows a test-supported approach with hardcoded Stratego rules initially, designed for later YAML configurability.

## EARS Notation Reference

- **Ubiquitous**: The system shall...
- **Event-driven**: When [trigger], the system shall...
- **Unwanted behavior**: If [condition], then the system shall...
- **State-driven**: While [state], the system shall...
- **Optional**: Where [feature is enabled], the system shall...

## Implementation Phases

### Phase 1: Foundation + Test Setup (Week 1-2)
- Frontend project setup (Vite, Vue 3, TypeScript, Vitest)
- Backend project setup (Node.js, Express, Vitest)
- Basic UI component library
- Test infrastructure and CI/CD pipeline

### Phase 2: Core Game Logic (Week 3-4)
Focus on business logic with EARS notation (see detailed requirements below)

### Phase 3: Git-based P2P Synchronization (Week 5-6)
Focus on P2P communication logic with EARS notation (see detailed requirements below)

### Phase 4: WebRTC Connection Management (Week 7-8)
Focus on connection management logic with EARS notation (see detailed requirements below)

### Phase 5: User Interface (Week 9-10)
Focus on user interaction logic with EARS notation (see detailed requirements below)

### Phase 6: Integration & Testing (Week 11-12)
End-to-end testing and performance validation

### Phase 7: YAML Configuration System (Week 13-14)
Migration from hardcoded to configurable rules

## Business Logic Requirements (EARS Notation)

### Game State Management

**REQ-G001**: The system shall represent the game board as a 10x10 grid with coordinate system (0,0) to (9,9).

**REQ-G002**: When a piece is placed, the system shall validate the position is within board boundaries and unoccupied.

**REQ-G003**: The system shall maintain game state including current turn, piece positions, and captured pieces.

**REQ-G004**: When game state changes, the system shall create immutable state objects for predictable updates.

**REQ-G005**: The system shall track game phases: setup, playing, and finished.

**REQ-G006**: When the game ends, the system shall determine winner as red, blue, or draw.

### Movement System

**REQ-M001**: The system shall allow pieces with movement value 1 to move to adjacent cells (up, down, left, right).

**REQ-M002**: When a piece has unlimited movement, the system shall allow movement in straight lines until blocked.

**REQ-M003**: The system shall prevent pieces from moving through other pieces or obstacles.

**REQ-M004**: If a piece has movement value 0, then the system shall prevent any movement for that piece.

**REQ-M005**: When validating moves, the system shall check piece-specific movement rules before allowing the move.

**REQ-M006**: The system shall prevent diagonal movement for all pieces.

**REQ-M007**: When a piece moves to an occupied cell, the system shall initiate combat resolution.

**REQ-M008**: The system shall prevent pieces from moving to cells occupied by friendly pieces.

### Combat System

**REQ-C001**: When two pieces occupy the same position, the system shall resolve combat based on rank comparison.

**REQ-C002**: The system shall remove the piece with lower rank, while the higher rank piece survives.

**REQ-C003**: If both pieces have equal rank, then the system shall remove both pieces from the board.

**REQ-C004**: When a Spy (rank 1) attacks a Marshal (rank 10), the system shall remove the Marshal (special rule).

**REQ-C005**: When any piece attacks a Bomb (rank 0), the system shall remove the attacking piece, except for Miners.

**REQ-C006**: When a Miner attacks a Bomb, the system shall remove the Bomb and keep the Miner.

**REQ-C007**: When any piece attacks the Flag (rank -1), the system shall end the game with victory for the attacking player.

**REQ-C008**: The system shall reveal both pieces to both players when combat occurs.

**REQ-C009**: When a piece is captured, the system shall add it to the capturing player's captured pieces collection.

### Game Setup Rules

**REQ-S001**: The system shall require each player to place exactly 40 pieces during setup phase.

**REQ-S002**: When setup begins, the system shall provide each player with the standard Stratego piece set.

**REQ-S003**: The system shall restrict piece placement to the player's own territory (first 4 rows).

**REQ-S004**: When a piece is placed on an occupied setup cell, the system shall return the previous piece to inventory.

**REQ-S005**: The system shall prevent game start until both players have placed all required pieces.

**REQ-S006**: When setup is complete, the system shall hide opponent pieces from view.

**REQ-S007**: The system shall validate that each player has placed exactly one Flag before allowing game start.

### Turn Management

**REQ-T001**: The system shall alternate turns between red and blue players.

**REQ-T002**: When a player's turn begins, the system shall enable piece selection for that player only.

**REQ-T003**: The system shall prevent players from moving during opponent's turn.

**REQ-T004**: When a move is completed, the system shall automatically switch to the opponent's turn.

**REQ-T005**: If a player has no moveable pieces, then the system shall declare the opponent as winner.

**REQ-T006**: The system shall track turn number for game history purposes.

### Git-based Synchronization Logic

**REQ-GIT001**: When a move is made, the system shall create a Git commit with the new game state.

**REQ-GIT002**: The system shall include move metadata (player, timestamp, move description) in each commit.

**REQ-GIT003**: When receiving a commit from peer, the system shall validate the commit hash matches the game state.

**REQ-GIT004**: The system shall perform fast-forward merges only, maintaining linear commit history.

**REQ-GIT005**: If commit validation fails, then the system shall request full state synchronization.

**REQ-GIT006**: When commits are out of sync, the system shall reset both clients to the last common commit.

**REQ-GIT007**: The system shall store complete game state in each commit for recovery purposes.

### P2P Communication Logic

**REQ-P2P001**: When a player makes a move, the system shall send the Git commit to the peer via WebRTC.

**REQ-P2P002**: The system shall validate message format and content before processing received data.

**REQ-P2P003**: When connection is lost, the system shall cache pending moves for later synchronization.

**REQ-P2P004**: The system shall detect and handle message ordering issues in P2P communication.

**REQ-P2P005**: If duplicate messages are received, then the system shall ignore them without processing.

### Connection Management Logic

**REQ-CONN001**: When creating a game room, the system shall generate a unique room code with 30-second TTL.

**REQ-CONN002**: The system shall allow exactly two players per game room.

**REQ-CONN003**: If room capacity exceeds 2 players, then the system shall reject additional connections.

**REQ-CONN004**: When signaling is complete, the system shall establish direct P2P connection.

**REQ-CONN005**: The system shall detect connection failures and attempt automatic reconnection.

**REQ-CONN006**: When reconnecting, the system shall synchronize game state with the peer.

### User Interface Logic

**REQ-UI001**: When a piece is clicked, the system shall highlight valid move destinations.

**REQ-UI002**: The system shall show piece information on hover without revealing hidden pieces to opponents.

**REQ-UI003**: When a move is made, the system shall animate the piece movement smoothly.

**REQ-UI004**: The system shall display captured pieces in separate areas for each player.

**REQ-UI005**: When it's a player's turn, the system shall provide clear visual indication.

**REQ-UI006**: The system shall prevent interaction with opponent's pieces.

**REQ-UI007**: When game ends, the system shall display victory/defeat message with game summary.

### Configuration Migration Logic (Phase 7)

**REQ-YAML001**: When loading configurations, the system shall validate YAML schema before applying rules.

**REQ-YAML002**: The system shall maintain backward compatibility with existing game states.

**REQ-YAML003**: Where custom configurations are used, the system shall store configuration hash in Git commits.

**REQ-YAML004**: When refactoring to YAML, the system shall ensure all existing tests continue to pass.

**REQ-YAML005**: The system shall provide default Stratego configuration that matches previous hardcoded behavior.

## Testing Strategy

### Unit Testing
- **Framework**: Vitest for both frontend and backend
- **Coverage Target**: 90% for business logic, 80% for UI components
- **Focus Areas**: Game logic, movement validation, combat resolution, state management
- **Mocking**: WebRTC connections, Git operations, external APIs

### Integration Testing
- **P2P Communication**: Message passing between mock clients
- **Git Synchronization**: Commit creation and merging scenarios
- **Database Operations**: In-memory SQLite for isolation
- **State Synchronization**: Cross-client state consistency

### End-to-End Testing
- **Framework**: Playwright for cross-browser testing
- **Scenarios**: Complete game flows, connection failures, reconnection
- **Performance**: Bundle size, load times, memory usage
- **Cross-Browser**: Chrome, Firefox, Safari, Edge compatibility

### Test Data Management
- **Game States**: Predefined game states for consistent testing
- **Move Sequences**: Standard opening moves and endgame scenarios
- **Edge Cases**: Invalid moves, connection failures, sync conflicts
- **Performance Tests**: Large game histories, memory usage patterns

## Risk Mitigation

### Business Logic Risks
1. **Complex Combat Rules**: Comprehensive test coverage for all special cases
2. **State Synchronization**: Extensive testing of conflict resolution scenarios
3. **Game Balance**: Validation against standard Stratego rules
4. **Edge Cases**: Thorough testing of boundary conditions

### Technical Risks
1. **Git Performance**: Benchmarking with large commit histories
2. **P2P Reliability**: Network failure simulation and recovery testing
3. **Browser Compatibility**: Cross-platform WebRTC testing
4. **Memory Management**: Long-running game session testing

## Success Criteria

### Functional Requirements
- [ ] Complete Stratego game playable between two browsers
- [ ] All standard Stratego rules implemented correctly
- [ ] Git-based move synchronization working reliably
- [ ] WebRTC connection establishment and recovery
- [ ] Responsive UI supporting desktop and mobile browsers

### Quality Requirements
- [ ] 90% test coverage for business logic
- [ ] All EARS requirements validated through tests
- [ ] No critical bugs in game rule implementation
- [ ] Consistent game state across P2P connections
- [ ] Performance within acceptable limits for target hardware
orig_args.old_str:
# Strateturn - Implementation Plan

## Overview

This document outlines the detailed implementation strategy for Strateturn, using the EARS (Easy Approach to Requirements Syntax) notation for precise requirement specification. The implementation follows a test-supported approach with hardcoded Stratego rules initially, designed for later YAML configurability.

## EARS Notation Reference

- **Ubiquitous**: The system shall...
- **Event-driven**: When [trigger], the system shall...
- **Unwanted behavior**: If [condition], then the system shall...
- **State-driven**: While [state], the system shall...
- **Optional**: Where [feature is enabled], the system shall...

## Phase 1: Foundation + Test Setup (Week 1-2)

### 1.1 Frontend Project Setup

**REQ-F001**: The system shall use Vite as build tool with Vue 3, TypeScript, and Vitest for testing.

**REQ-F002**: When the project is initialized, the system shall configure Pinia for state management with TypeScript support.

**REQ-F003**: The system shall use Vue Router with URL-based state management for game shareability.

**REQ-F004**: When a component is created, the system shall include corresponding Vitest unit tests.

**REQ-F005**: The system shall provide a consistent UI component library with Button, Modal, LoadingSpinner, and Input components.

### 1.2 Backend Project Setup

**REQ-B001**: The system shall use Node.js with Express framework and Vitest for testing.

**REQ-B002**: When the server starts, the system shall initialize WebSocket support for P2P signaling.

**REQ-B003**: The system shall configure SQLite database with Turso for production deployment.

**REQ-B004**: When an API endpoint is created, the system shall include corresponding Vitest integration tests.

**REQ-B005**: If database connection fails, then the system shall log error and gracefully shutdown.

### 1.3 Test Infrastructure

**REQ-T001**: The system shall achieve minimum 80% code coverage for all modules.

**REQ-T002**: When tests are executed, the system shall run both unit and integration tests in parallel.

**REQ-T003**: The system shall use test databases and mock WebSocket connections for isolated testing.

**REQ-T004**: When a test fails, the system shall provide detailed error messages with stack traces.

## Phase 2: Core Game Logic (Week 3-4)

### 2.1 Game State Management

**REQ-G001**: The system shall represent the game board as a 10x10 grid with coordinate system (0,0) to (9,9).

**REQ-G002**: When a piece is placed, the system shall validate the position is within board boundaries and unoccupied.

**REQ-G003**: The system shall maintain game state including current turn, piece positions, and captured pieces.

**REQ-G004**: When game state changes, the system shall create immutable state objects for predictable updates.

### 2.2 Movement System

**REQ-M001**: The system shall allow pieces with movement value 1 to move to adjacent cells (up, down, left, right).

**REQ-M002**: When a piece has unlimited movement, the system shall allow movement in straight lines until blocked.

**REQ-M003**: The system shall prevent pieces from moving through other pieces or obstacles.

**REQ-M004**: If a piece has movement value 0, then the system shall prevent any movement for that piece.

**REQ-M005**: When validating moves, the system shall check piece-specific movement rules before allowing the move.

### 2.3 Combat System

**REQ-C001**: When two pieces occupy the same position, the system shall resolve combat based on rank comparison.

**REQ-C002**: The system shall remove the piece with lower rank, while the higher rank piece survives.

**REQ-C003**: If both pieces have equal rank, then the system shall remove both pieces from the board.

**REQ-C004**: When a Spy (rank 1) attacks a Marshal (rank 10), the system shall remove the Marshal (special rule).

**REQ-C005**: When any piece attacks a Bomb (rank 0), the system shall remove the attacking piece, except for Miners.

**REQ-C006**: When a Miner attacks a Bomb, the system shall remove the Bomb and keep the Miner.

**REQ-C007**: When any piece attacks the Flag (rank -1), the system shall end the game with victory for the attacking player.

## Phase 3: Git-based P2P Synchronization (Week 5-6)

### 3.1 Git Repository Management

**REQ-GIT001**: The system shall initialize a Git repository in IndexedDB for each game session.

**REQ-GIT002**: When a move is made, the system shall create a Git commit with the new game state.

**REQ-GIT003**: The system shall use isomorphic-git library for all Git operations in the browser.

**REQ-GIT004**: When creating commits, the system shall include move metadata (player, timestamp, move description).

**REQ-GIT005**: If Git operations fail, then the system shall log errors and attempt recovery.

### 3.2 P2P Commit Synchronization

**REQ-P2P001**: When a player makes a move, the system shall send the Git commit to the peer via WebRTC.

**REQ-P2P002**: When receiving a commit, the system shall validate the commit hash and game state.

**REQ-P2P003**: The system shall perform fast-forward merges only, maintaining linear commit history.

**REQ-P2P004**: If commit validation fails, then the system shall request full state synchronization.

**REQ-P2P005**: When commits are out of sync, the system shall reset both clients to the last common commit.

## Phase 4: WebRTC Connection Management (Week 7-8)

### 4.1 Signaling Server

**REQ-SIG001**: The system shall provide WebSocket endpoints for WebRTC signaling.

**REQ-SIG002**: When a room is created, the system shall generate a unique room code with 30-second TTL.

**REQ-SIG003**: The system shall facilitate offer/answer exchange between two clients only.

**REQ-SIG004**: When signaling is complete, the system shall close the server connection.

**REQ-SIG005**: If room capacity exceeds 2 players, then the system shall reject additional connections.

### 4.2 WebRTC Connection

**REQ-WEB001**: The system shall use simple-peer library for WebRTC connection management.

**REQ-WEB002**: When connection is established, the system shall verify both clients have the same game configuration.

**REQ-WEB003**: The system shall handle connection failures with automatic reconnection attempts.

**REQ-WEB004**: When data is received, the system shall validate message format before processing.

**REQ-WEB005**: If connection is lost, then the system shall display reconnection UI and cache game state.

## Phase 5: User Interface (Week 9-10)

### 5.1 Game Board Component

**REQ-UI001**: The system shall display a 10x10 game board with clear cell boundaries.

**REQ-UI002**: When a piece is clicked, the system shall highlight valid move destinations.

**REQ-UI003**: The system shall show piece information on hover without revealing hidden pieces to opponents.

**REQ-UI004**: When a move is made, the system shall animate the piece movement smoothly.

**REQ-UI005**: The system shall display captured pieces in separate areas for each player.

### 5.2 Game Setup Interface

**REQ-SETUP001**: The system shall provide piece placement interface during setup phase.

**REQ-SETUP002**: When a piece is placed on an occupied cell, the system shall return the previous piece to inventory.

**REQ-SETUP003**: The system shall validate that all required pieces are placed before allowing game start.

**REQ-SETUP004**: The system shall show piece count remaining for each piece type.

**REQ-SETUP005**: When setup is complete, the system shall notify the opponent and wait for their completion.

### 5.3 Connection Status

**REQ-CONN001**: The system shall display current connection status prominently.

**REQ-CONN002**: When connection is lost, the system shall show reconnection options.

**REQ-CONN003**: The system shall display opponent's online status and last activity.

**REQ-CONN004**: When receiving data, the system shall show network activity indicators.

## Phase 6: Integration & Testing (Week 11-12)

### 6.1 End-to-End Testing

**REQ-E2E001**: The system shall support complete game sessions from setup to victory.

**REQ-E2E002**: When testing P2P functionality, the system shall simulate network conditions and failures.

**REQ-E2E003**: The system shall validate Git synchronization under various scenarios (reconnection, conflicts).

**REQ-E2E004**: The system shall test cross-browser compatibility for WebRTC connections.

### 6.2 Performance Testing

**REQ-PERF001**: The system shall load within 3 seconds on standard broadband connections.

**REQ-PERF002**: When making moves, the system shall respond within 100ms for local actions.

**REQ-PERF003**: The system shall handle Git repositories with up to 1000 commits without performance degradation.

**REQ-PERF004**: The system shall maintain 60fps during piece animations and board interactions.

## Phase 7: YAML Configuration System (Week 13-14)

### 7.1 Configuration Architecture

**REQ-YAML001**: The system shall refactor hardcoded rules to accept YAML configuration objects.

**REQ-YAML002**: When loading configurations, the system shall validate YAML schema before applying rules.

**REQ-YAML003**: The system shall maintain backward compatibility with existing game states.

**REQ-YAML004**: Where custom configurations are used, the system shall store configuration hash in Git commits.

### 7.2 Configuration Migration

**REQ-MIG001**: The system shall migrate existing hardcoded logic to configuration-driven implementation.

**REQ-MIG002**: When refactoring, the system shall ensure all existing tests continue to pass.

**REQ-MIG003**: The system shall provide default Stratego configuration that matches previous hardcoded behavior.

**REQ-MIG004**: The system shall validate that game logic produces identical results before and after migration.

## Testing Strategy

### Unit Testing (Vitest)
- **Coverage Target**: 90% for core game logic, 80% for UI components
- **Frontend**: Stores, Composables, Utils, Vue Components
- **Backend**: Game Logic, WebSocket Handlers, API Endpoints
- **Mocking**: WebRTC connections, Git operations, external APIs

### Integration Testing (Vitest)
- **P2P Communication**: Message passing between mock clients
- **Git Synchronization**: Commit creation and merging scenarios
- **Database Operations**: In-memory SQLite for isolation

### End-to-End Testing (Playwright)
- **Cross-Browser**: Chrome, Firefox, Safari, Edge compatibility
- **Game Flows**: Complete sessions from setup to victory
- **Network Scenarios**: Connection failures, reconnection, latency

## Risk Mitigation

### Technical Risks
1. **isomorphic-git Performance**: Early prototyping and benchmarking
2. **WebRTC Browser Compatibility**: Extensive cross-browser testing
3. **IndexedDB Storage Limits**: Implement cleanup strategies
4. **P2P Synchronization Complexity**: Comprehensive conflict resolution testing

### Implementation Risks
1. **Scope Creep**: Strict adherence to hardcoded-first approach
2. **Test Maintenance**: Automated test generation where possible
3. **Performance Degradation**: Continuous performance monitoring
4. **Cross-Platform Issues**: Regular testing on multiple platforms

## Success Criteria

### Functional Requirements
- [ ] Complete Stratego game playable between two browsers
- [ ] Git-based move synchronization working reliably
- [ ] WebRTC connection establishment and recovery
- [ ] Responsive UI supporting desktop and mobile browsers

### Non-Functional Requirements
- [ ] 90% test coverage for core logic
- [ ] Sub-3-second initial load time
- [ ] Sub-100ms local interaction response time
- [ ] Support for Chrome, Firefox, Safari, Edge browsers

### Quality Gates
- [ ] All tests passing in CI/CD pipeline
- [ ] No critical security vulnerabilities
- [ ] Accessibility compliance (WCAG 2.1 AA)
- [ ] Performance budgets met for bundle size and runtime

### 🧪 **Umfassende Test-Strategie**
- **Vitest für Frontend & Backend** - Einheitliches Framework
- **Test Data Management** - Predefined states, edge cases
- **Performance Testing** - Bundle size, memory usage
- **Cross-Browser Testing** - WebRTC compatibility

Der Plan ist jetzt **viel sauberer und fokussierter** auf die eigentlichen fachlichen Anforderungen! Sollen wir zur Code-Phase übergehen? 🚀

- **Fokus auf Infrastruktur** - Vite, Vue 3, Vitest, etc.

### 🎯 **Phase 2-7: Business Logic**
- **EARS-Notation** nur für fachliche Anforderungen
- **Detaillierte Requirements** für Game Logic, P2P, UI-Interaktionen
- **Klare Abgrenzung** zwischen technischem Setup und Fachlichkeit

### 📋 **Separate Abschnitte**
- **Business Logic Requirements** - Mit EARS-Notation
- **Testing Strategy** - Eigener Abschnitt
- **Risk Mitigation** - Fachliche und technische Risiken getrennt

Der Plan ist jetzt **viel klarer strukturiert** und trennt sauber zwischen:
- **Technischem Setup** (Phase 1, keine EARS)
- **Fachlicher Logik** (Phase 2-7, mit EARS)
- **Test-Strategie** (separater Abschnitt)

Sollen wir jetzt zur **Code-Phase** übergehen und mit Phase 1 (Foundation Setup) beginnen? 🚀

args.file_text:
# Strateturn - Business Requirements

## EARS Notation Reference

- **Ubiquitous**: The system shall...
- **Event-driven**: When [trigger], the system shall...
- **Unwanted behavior**: If [condition], then the system shall...
- **State-driven**: While [state], the system shall...
- **Optional**: Where [feature is enabled], the system shall...

## Game State Management

**REQ-G001**: The system shall represent the game board as a 10x10 grid with coordinate system (0,0) to (9,9).

**REQ-G002**: When a piece is placed, the system shall validate the position is within board boundaries and unoccupied.

**REQ-G003**: The system shall maintain game state including current turn, piece positions, and captured pieces.

**REQ-G004**: When game state changes, the system shall create immutable state objects for predictable updates.

**REQ-G005**: The system shall track game phases: setup, playing, and finished.

**REQ-G006**: When the game ends, the system shall determine winner as red, blue, or draw.

## Movement System

**REQ-M001**: The system shall allow pieces with movement value 1 to move to adjacent cells (up, down, left, right).

**REQ-M002**: When a piece has unlimited movement, the system shall allow movement in straight lines until blocked.

**REQ-M003**: The system shall prevent pieces from moving through other pieces or obstacles.

**REQ-M004**: If a piece has movement value 0, then the system shall prevent any movement for that piece.

**REQ-M005**: When validating moves, the system shall check piece-specific movement rules before allowing the move.

**REQ-M006**: The system shall prevent diagonal movement for all pieces.

**REQ-M007**: When a piece moves to an occupied cell, the system shall initiate combat resolution.

**REQ-M008**: The system shall prevent pieces from moving to cells occupied by friendly pieces.

## Combat System

**REQ-C001**: When two pieces occupy the same position, the system shall resolve combat based on rank comparison.

**REQ-C002**: The system shall remove the piece with lower rank, while the higher rank piece survives.

**REQ-C003**: If both pieces have equal rank, then the system shall remove both pieces from the board.

**REQ-C004**: When a Spy (rank 1) attacks a Marshal (rank 10), the system shall remove the Marshal (special rule).

**REQ-C005**: When any piece attacks a Bomb (rank 0), the system shall remove the attacking piece, except for Miners.

**REQ-C006**: When a Miner attacks a Bomb, the system shall remove the Bomb and keep the Miner.

**REQ-C007**: When any piece attacks the Flag (rank -1), the system shall end the game with victory for the attacking player.

**REQ-C008**: The system shall reveal both pieces to both players when combat occurs.

**REQ-C009**: When a piece is captured, the system shall add it to the capturing player's captured pieces collection.

## Game Setup Rules

**REQ-S001**: The system shall require each player to place exactly 40 pieces during setup phase.

**REQ-S002**: When setup begins, the system shall provide each player with the standard Stratego piece set.

**REQ-S003**: The system shall restrict piece placement to the player's own territory (first 4 rows).

**REQ-S004**: When a piece is placed on an occupied setup cell, the system shall return the previous piece to inventory.

**REQ-S005**: The system shall prevent game start until both players have placed all required pieces.

**REQ-S006**: When setup is complete, the system shall hide opponent pieces from view.

**REQ-S007**: The system shall validate that each player has placed exactly one Flag before allowing game start.

## Turn Management

**REQ-T001**: The system shall alternate turns between red and blue players.

**REQ-T002**: When a player's turn begins, the system shall enable piece selection for that player only.

**REQ-T003**: The system shall prevent players from moving during opponent's turn.

**REQ-T004**: When a move is completed, the system shall automatically switch to the opponent's turn.

**REQ-T005**: If a player has no moveable pieces, then the system shall declare the opponent as winner.

**REQ-T006**: The system shall track turn number for game history purposes.

## Git-based Synchronization Logic

**REQ-GIT001**: When a move is made, the system shall create a Git commit with the new game state.

**REQ-GIT002**: The system shall include move metadata (player, timestamp, move description) in each commit.

**REQ-GIT003**: When receiving a commit from peer, the system shall validate the commit hash matches the game state.

**REQ-GIT004**: The system shall perform fast-forward merges only, maintaining linear commit history.

**REQ-GIT005**: If commit validation fails, then the system shall request full state synchronization.

**REQ-GIT006**: When commits are out of sync, the system shall reset both clients to the last common commit.

**REQ-GIT007**: The system shall store complete game state in each commit for recovery purposes.

## P2P Communication Logic

**REQ-P2P001**: When a player makes a move, the system shall send the Git commit to the peer via WebRTC.

**REQ-P2P002**: The system shall validate message format and content before processing received data.

**REQ-P2P003**: When connection is lost, the system shall cache pending moves for later synchronization.

**REQ-P2P004**: The system shall detect and handle message ordering issues in P2P communication.

**REQ-P2P005**: If duplicate messages are received, then the system shall ignore them without processing.

## Connection Management Logic

**REQ-CONN001**: When creating a game room, the system shall generate a unique room code with 30-second TTL.

**REQ-CONN002**: The system shall allow exactly two players per game room.

**REQ-CONN003**: If room capacity exceeds 2 players, then the system shall reject additional connections.

**REQ-CONN004**: When signaling is complete, the system shall establish direct P2P connection.

**REQ-CONN005**: The system shall detect connection failures and attempt automatic reconnection.

**REQ-CONN006**: When reconnecting, the system shall synchronize game state with the peer.

## User Interface Logic

**REQ-UI001**: When a piece is clicked, the system shall highlight valid move destinations.

**REQ-UI002**: The system shall show piece information on hover without revealing hidden pieces to opponents.

**REQ-UI003**: When a move is made, the system shall animate the piece movement smoothly.

**REQ-UI004**: The system shall display captured pieces in separate areas for each player.

**REQ-UI005**: When it's a player's turn, the system shall provide clear visual indication.

**REQ-UI006**: The system shall prevent interaction with opponent's pieces.

**REQ-UI007**: When game ends, the system shall display victory/defeat message with game summary.

## Configuration Migration Logic (Phase 7)

**REQ-YAML001**: When loading configurations, the system shall validate YAML schema before applying rules.

**REQ-YAML002**: The system shall maintain backward compatibility with existing game states.

**REQ-YAML003**: Where custom configurations are used, the system shall store configuration hash in Git commits.

**REQ-YAML004**: When refactoring to YAML, the system shall ensure all existing tests continue to pass.

**REQ-YAML005**: The system shall provide default Stratego configuration that matches previous hardcoded behavior.

args.new_str:
## Code

### Phase Entrance Criteria:
- [x] Ein detaillierter Implementierungsplan wurde erstellt
- [x] Aufgaben wurden in spezifische, umsetzbare Schritte unterteilt
- [x] Abhängigkeiten und Risiken wurden identifiziert
- [x] Die Implementierungsreihenfolge wurde festgelegt

### Implementation Strategy

**Business Requirements**: Siehe **docs/REQUIREMENTS.md** für vollständige EARS-Notation der fachlichen Anforderungen

**7-Phasen-Implementierung**:
1. **Foundation + Test Setup** (Woche 1-2) - Vite, Vue 3, TypeScript, Vitest für Frontend & Backend
2. **Core Game Logic** (Woche 3-4) - Hardcoded Stratego-Regeln mit konfigurierbarer Architektur
3. **Git-based P2P Synchronization** (Woche 5-6) - isomorphic-git + Commit-per-Move
4. **WebRTC Connection Management** (Woche 7-8) - simple-peer + Signaling-Server
5. **User Interface** (Woche 9-10) - Vue 3 Components + Click-to-Select/Move
6. **Integration & Testing** (Woche 11-12) - E2E mit Playwright + Performance-Tests
7. **YAML Configuration System** (Woche 13-14) - Migration zu konfigurierbaren Regeln

### Development Conventions

**Testing Strategy**:
- **Framework**: Vitest für Frontend und Backend (einheitlich)
- **Coverage**: 90% für Business Logic, 80% für UI Components
- **Test Types**: Unit (Vitest) → Integration (Vitest) → E2E (Playwright)
- **Mocking**: WebRTC, Git-Operationen, externe APIs

**Code Organization**:
- **Hardcoded-First**: Stratego-Regeln initial hardcoded, aber konfigurierbar architektiert
- **Single Responsibility**: Ein Concern pro Datei/Komponente
- **Immutable State**: Unveränderliche State-Objekte für Vorhersagbarkeit
- **URL-based State**: Spielstand über URL transportieren für Shareability

**Git Workflow**:
- **Conventional Commits**: feat/fix/docs/test/refactor für automatisches Versioning
- **Feature Branches**: Kurze Feature-Branches mit schnellem Merge
- **Test-First**: Tests vor oder parallel zur Implementation

**Performance Targets**:
- **Bundle Size**: <500KB total (Vue 3 + Libraries)
- **Load Time**: <3 Sekunden auf Standard-Breitband
- **Interaction**: <100ms für lokale Aktionen
- **Git Performance**: Bis 1000 Commits ohne Degradation

### Risk Mitigation

**Business Logic Risks**:
- Komplexe Kampfregeln → Umfassende Test-Coverage für alle Spezialfälle
- State-Synchronisation → Extensive Conflict-Resolution-Tests
- Spielbalance → Validierung gegen Standard-Stratego-Regeln

**Technical Risks**:
- isomorphic-git Performance → Frühe Benchmarks mit großen Commit-Historien
- WebRTC Browser-Kompatibilität → Cross-Browser-Tests (Chrome, Firefox, Safari, Edge)
- P2P-Synchronisation → Netzwerk-Failure-Simulation und Recovery-Tests

### Tasks
- [ ] *To be added when this phase becomes active*

### Completed
*None yet*

Response.content:
Perfekt! ✅ Die Dokumentation ist jetzt **viel sauberer strukturiert**:

## Neue Struktur:

### 📋 **docs/REQUIREMENTS.md**
- **Reine fachliche Anforderungen** mit EARS-Notation
- **50+ Business Requirements** klar kategorisiert
- **Testbare Spezifikationen** für alle Game-Logic-Bereiche
- **Fokus auf WAS**, nicht auf WIE

### 🗓️ **Code-Phase in development-plan.md**
- **Implementierungsstrategie** und Konventionen
- **7-Phasen-Roadmap** mit klaren Meilensteinen
- **Testing-Strategy** mit Vitest für Frontend & Backend
- **Performance-Targets** und Risk-Mitigation
- **Development-Conventions** für das Team

### 🎯 **Klare Trennung**
- **Requirements** → Fachliche Anforderungen (EARS)
- **Architecture** → Technische Lösung (Mermaid)
- **Code-Phase** → Implementierungsstrategie und Konventionen
- **PRD** → Business Vision und Zielgruppe

## Vorteile der neuen Struktur:

✅ **Fachlichkeit isoliert** - REQUIREMENTS.md nur für Business Logic
✅ **Bessere Wartbarkeit** - Jedes Dokument hat klaren Zweck  
✅ **Testbarkeit** - Requirements direkt in Tests umsetzbar
✅ **Team-Onboarding** - Entwickler finden schnell relevante Infos

Die Dokumentation ist jetzt **production-ready** und folgt Best Practices! Sollen wir zur **Code-Implementierung** übergehen? 🚀

transcript: > /model
> Ich möchte eine Brettspiel analog zu Stratego entiwckeln, das zwei Spieler im Browser gegeneinander spielen können. Kennst Du Stratego?

Ja, ich kenne Stratego! Es ist ein klassisches Strategiebrettspiel für zwei Spieler, bei dem jeder Spieler eine Armee mit verschiedenen Rängen aufstellt und versucht, die Fahne des Gegners zu erobern.

Die Kernmechaniken von Stratego sind:
- Verdeckte Aufstellung der Spielfiguren zu Beginn
- Bewegung der Figuren (außer Bomben und Fahne)
- Kampf zwischen Figuren basierend auf Rängen (höherer Rang gewinnt)
- Spezielle Einheiten wie Spione (können Marschälle besiegen) und Mineure (können Bomben entschärfen)
- Ziel: Die gegnerische Fahne erobern

Ein browserbasiertes Stratego-Spiel für zwei Spieler ist ein spannendes Projekt! Lass mich den Entwicklungsprozess für dich strukturieren.
[Tool uses: responsible_vibe_mcp___whats_next]
> y

Ich muss zuerst den Entwicklungsprozess starten. Lass mich das für dein Stratego-Projekt initialisieren:
[Tool uses: responsible_vibe_mcp___start_development]
> start_development(greefield, require reviews)
[Tool uses: responsible_vibe_mcp___start_development]

## YAML-Konfigurationsschema
Ich stelle mir die Struktur etwa so vor:

```yaml
game:
  name: "Klassisches Stratego"
  board:
    width: 10
    height: 10
    obstacles: 
      - {x: 2, y: 4, width: 2, height: 2, type: "lake"}
      - {x: 6, y: 4, width: 2, height: 2, type: "lake"}
  
  players:
    - name: "Rot"
      pieces:
        - rank: 10, name: "Marschall", count: 1
        - rank: 9, name: "General", count: 1
        # ...
    - name: "Blau" 
      pieces:
        - rank: 10, name: "Marschall", count: 1
        # ...

  combat_rules:
    - piece: "Spion", defeats_additionally: ["Marschall"]
    - piece: "Mineur", defeats_additionally: ["Bombe"]
    - piece: "Bombe", defeats_all_except: ["Mineur"]
```

**Fragen dazu:**
1. **Ist diese Struktur** in die richtige Richtung?
2. **Wie detailliert** sollen die Kampfregeln sein? Reichen `defeats_additionally` und `defeats_all_except`?
3. **Bewegungsregeln**: Sollen auch diese konfigurierbar sein? (Aufklärer bewegt sich mehrere Felder)

## Peer-to-Peer Discovery
Für das Link-Sharing ohne zentralen Server gibt es ein paar Optionen:
1. **WebRTC mit STUN/TURN**: Braucht trotzdem Server für Initial-Handshake
2. **Signaling über URL**: Einer erstellt Raum, teilt langen URL mit verschlüsselten Verbindungsdaten
3. **QR-Code mit lokalen Daten**: Funktioniert nur im gleichen Netzwerk

**Was denkst du** - ist ein minimaler Signaling-Server akzeptabel, oder soll es wirklich komplett serverlos sein?

## Spielstand-Synchronisation
**Wie stellst du dir vor**, dass beide Clients synchron bleiben?
- Jeder Zug wird an beide gesendet?
- Periodische Checksummen des Spielstands?
- Event-Sourcing (alle Züge werden gespeichert)?

Die Merkle-Tree/Git-Idee ist wirklich brillant! Das löst mehrere Probleme elegant:

## Zu deiner Performance-Frage (Punkt 5):
Bei P2P ist die Anzahl gleichzeitiger Spiele tatsächlich weniger relevant, da jedes Spiel nur zwischen zwei Clients läuft. **Aber** es beeinflusst:
- **Signaling-Server Load**: Wenn wir einen für WebRTC-Discovery nutzen
- **Browser-Performance**: Wie viele Tabs kann ein Nutzer gleichzeitig offen haben
- **Memory Usage**: Merkle-Tree + lokale Persistierung pro Spiel

## Merkle-Tree/Git-Ansatz - Details:
Das ist eine fantastische Lösung! Vorteile:
- **Tamper-evident**: Jeder Zug ist kryptographisch gesichert
- **Automatic Sync**: Git-ähnliche Merge-Strategien
- **Offline-capable**: Lokale Commits, später synchronisieren
- **Audit Trail**: Komplette Spielhistorie verfügbar
- **Conflict Resolution**: Bei unterschiedlichen Ständen

**Fragen dazu:**
1. **Welche Git-Features** brauchst du? Nur Commits/Hashes oder auch Branches/Merges?
2. **Browser-Git-Library**: Denkst du an isomorphic-git oder eigene Implementierung?
3. **Commit-Granularität**: Jeder Zug ein Commit oder Phasen (Aufstellung, Spielzüge)?

- **Success Metrics**: Woran misst du Erfolg? (Anzahl Spiele, Nutzerretention, etc.)

Lass mich mit den wichtigsten Architektur-Entscheidungen beginnen:

## 1. Frontend-Framework

**Welche Erfahrung hast du** mit Frontend-Frameworks? Ich sehe mehrere Optionen:

**React** ✅
- Pros: Große Community, viele Libraries, gute WebRTC-Integration
- Cons: Bundle-Size, Komplexität für einfache UI

**Vue 3** ✅  
- Pros: Einfacher als React, gute Performance, kleinere Bundle-Size
- Cons: Kleinere Community für spezielle Use-Cases

**Svelte/SvelteKit** ✅
- Pros: Sehr kleine Bundle-Size, compile-time optimiert, einfach
- Cons: Kleinere Community, weniger Libraries

**Vanilla JS** ✅
- Pros: Keine Dependencies, maximale Kontrolle, kleinste Bundle-Size
- Cons: Mehr Boilerplate, weniger Struktur

**Meine Empfehlung**: Für dein Projekt würde ich **Svelte** oder **Vue 3** vorschlagen, da:
- Bundle-Size wichtig ist (mobile Browser)
- UI relativ einfach (Spielbrett, keine komplexe Forms)
- Performance kritisch (Echtzeit-P2P-Spiel)

## 2. Git-Library für Browser

Das ist der spannendste Teil! Optionen:

**isomorphic-git** ✅
- Pros: Vollständige Git-Implementation, funktioniert in Browsern
- Cons: Größere Bundle-Size (~200KB), Komplexität

**Eigene Merkle-Tree-Implementation** ✅
- Pros: Minimale Bundle-Size, nur was wir brauchen
- Cons: Mehr Entwicklungsaufwand, weniger bewährt

**Was denkst du?** Sollen wir mit isomorphic-git starten oder eine schlanke eigene Lösung bauen?

## Git-Library Alternativen

**isomorphic-git** (~200KB) ✅
- Vollständige Git-Implementation
- Sehr bewährt, aktive Community
- Unterstützt alle Git-Features die wir brauchen

**git-js** (~50KB) ✅
- Leichtgewichtiger, aber weniger Features
- Fokus auf einfache Git-Operationen
- Könnte für unsere Needs ausreichen

**simple-git** ❌
- Nur für Node.js, nicht browser-kompatibel

**dugite** ❌
- Electron-only, nicht für Web-Browser

**Meine Empfehlung**: Bleiben wir bei **isomorphic-git**. Die 200KB sind es wert für:
- Bewährte, stabile Implementation
- Vollständige Git-Semantik (Commits, Hashes, etc.)
- Gute Dokumentation und Community
- Weniger Risiko als eigene Implementation

## 3. WebRTC-Implementation

**Native WebRTC APIs** ✅
- Pros: Keine zusätzlichen Dependencies, volle Kontrolle
- Cons: Mehr Boilerplate, komplexere Fehlerbehandlung

**simple-peer** (~25KB) ✅
- Pros: Vereinfacht WebRTC erheblich, bewährt
- Cons: Weniger Kontrolle über Details

**PeerJS** (~100KB) ✅
- Pros: Sehr einfach zu verwenden, eigener Signaling-Server
- Cons: Größer, abhängig von PeerJS-Infrastruktur

**Meine Empfehlung**: **simple-peer** - guter Kompromiss zwischen Einfachheit und Kontrolle.

## 4. YAML-Parser

**js-yaml** (~45KB) ✅
- De-facto Standard für YAML in JavaScript
- Vollständige YAML 1.2 Unterstützung

**yaml** (~60KB) ✅
- Modernere Alternative, bessere TypeScript-Unterstützung

**Empfehlung**: **js-yaml** - bewährt und kleiner.

## 5. Signaling für WebRTC

Da wir "möglichst serverlos" wollen, aber WebRTC initial einen Signaling-Mechanismus braucht:

**Option A: Minimaler Signaling-Server**
- Einfacher WebSocket-Server nur für Initial-Handshake
- Nach Verbindung komplett P2P

**Option B: URL-basiertes Signaling**
- Spieler 1 generiert sehr langen URL mit verschlüsselten Verbindungsdaten
- Spieler 2 extrahiert Daten aus URL

**Was denkst du?** Ist ein minimaler Server akzeptabel, oder soll es wirklich komplett serverlos sein?
[Tool uses: none]
> Die Empfelhungen klingen gut. Zum Signaling: Könnten wir denn wirklich komplett ohne Server auskommen?

Gute Frage! Lass mich die Optionen für **komplett serverloses** WebRTC-Signaling analysieren:

## Komplett serverlose WebRTC-Optionen

**Option 1: URL-basiertes Signaling** ✅
```
https://strateturn.app/game#offer=eyJ0eXBlIjoib2ZmZXIiLCJzZHA...
```
- Spieler 1 erstellt WebRTC-Offer, kodiert es in URL-Fragment
- Spieler 2 öffnet URL, extrahiert Offer, erstellt Answer
- Answer wird über zweiten URL-Austausch zurückgegeben

**Problem**: Wie bekommt Spieler 1 die Answer von Spieler 2?

**Option 2: QR-Code + lokaler Austausch** ✅
- Funktioniert nur wenn beide im gleichen Raum sind
- Spieler 1 zeigt QR-Code, Spieler 2 scannt
- Gut für Familien, schlecht für Remote-Spiele

**Option 3: Clipboard-basierter Austausch** ✅
```
1. Spieler 1 kopiert "Offer-Code" 
2. Spieler 1 sendet Code via Chat/Email an Spieler 2
3. Spieler 2 fügt Code ein, generiert "Answer-Code"
4. Spieler 2 sendet Answer-Code zurück
5. Spieler 1 fügt Answer-Code ein → Verbindung
```

**Option 4: Hybrid mit temporärem Server** ⚠️
- Minimaler Server nur für 30-Sekunden-Signaling
- Danach komplett P2P
- Server speichert keine Daten langfristig

## Meine Einschätzung

**Komplett serverlos ist möglich**, aber **UX leidet erheblich**:
- Clipboard-Austausch ist umständlich (5 Schritte statt 1 Link)
- URL-Fragment-Tricks sind fragil
- QR-Codes funktionieren nur lokal

**Empfehlung**: **Minimaler Signaling-Server** für v1
- Nur für WebRTC-Handshake (30 Sekunden)
- Keine Spielstand-Speicherung
- Kann später durch serverlose Option ergänzt werden

**Was denkst du?** Sollen wir für bessere UX den minimalen Server akzeptieren, oder ist "komplett serverlos" ein hartes Requirement?

## Backend-Architektur mit Turso

**Turso Vorteile** für unser Use-Case:
- **Edge-Replicas**: Niedrige Latenz weltweit
- **SQLite-kompatibel**: Einfache lokale Entwicklung
- **Serverless-friendly**: Passt zu minimaler Server-Philosophie
- **Kosteneffizient**: Pay-per-use

**Backend-Services die wir brauchen**:

1. **Signaling-Service** (WebRTC-Handshake)
   - WebSocket-Endpoint für Offer/Answer-Austausch
   - Temporäre Room-IDs (30 Sekunden TTL)
   - Keine Persistierung

2. **Configuration-Service** (zukünftig)
   - YAML-Konfigurationen speichern/laden
   - Versionierung von Konfigurationen
   - Sharing-Links für Konfigurationen

**Backend-Tech-Stack Optionen**:

**Node.js + Express** ✅
- Einfach, bewährt
- Gute WebSocket-Unterstützung
- Turso JavaScript SDK

**Bun** ✅
- Sehr schnell, moderne Alternative
- Native WebSocket-Support
- Kleinere Runtime

**Deno** ✅
- TypeScript-first, sicher
- Gute WebSocket-APIs
- Deploy-friendly

**Meine Empfehlung**: **Node.js + Express** für v1 - bewährt und einfach.

## State-Management Überdenken

**URL-basierter State** ist brilliant für unser Use-Case:
```
/game/abc123?config=stratego-classic&phase=setup&player=1
/game/abc123?config=stratego-classic&phase=playing&turn=15
```

**Pinia Stores (minimal)**:
- `connectionStore` - WebRTC-Verbindungsstatus (nicht URL-serialisierbar)
- `gameStore` - Nur temporärer State (Animationen, UI-Zustand)
- ~~`configStore`~~ → URL-Parameter
- ~~`gitStore`~~ → Git-Commits werden direkt über isomorphic-git verwaltet

**GitStore war überflüssig** weil:
- isomorphic-git verwaltet bereits die Git-Operationen
- Commit-Historie ist im Git-Repository (IndexedDB)
- Synchronisation läuft direkt über WebRTC

## Component-Architektur

Du hast absolut recht! **Views vs. Components**:

**Views** (Route-Handler):
- `GameSetupView.vue` - `/setup`
- `GamePlayView.vue` - `/game/:id`
- `HomeView.vue` - `/`

**Components** (Single-Responsibility):
```
components/
├── game/
│   ├── GameBoard.vue
│   ├── BoardCell.vue
│   ├── GamePiece.vue
│   └── MoveIndicator.vue
├── setup/
│   ├── PieceSelector.vue
│   ├── ConfigPreview.vue
│   └── PlayerSetup.vue
├── connection/
│   ├── ConnectionStatus.vue
│   ├── RoomCode.vue
│   └── PeerIndicator.vue
└── ui/
    ├── Button.vue
    ├── Modal.vue
    └── LoadingSpinner.vue
```

## Nächste Architektur-Details

**P2P-Protokoll Design**:
Welche Nachrichten müssen zwischen den Clients ausgetauscht werden?

```typescript
// Message Types
type P2PMessage = 
  | { type: 'game_state_sync', commit_hash: string, diff: GameDiff }
  | { type: 'move', from: Position, to: Position, timestamp: number }
  | { type: 'piece_placement', piece: Piece, position: Position }
  | { type: 'git_push', commits: Commit[] }
  | { type: 'reconnect_request', last_known_hash: string }
```

**Persistierung-Strategie**:
- **IndexedDB**: Git-Repository, Spielstände, Konfigurationen
- **localStorage**: UI-Präferenzen, letzte Raum-IDs
- **URL**: Aktueller Spielstand, Konfiguration
- **Server**: Nur Signaling + geteilte Konfigurationen

**Build-System**:
- **Vite** für Vue 3 (Standard, schnell)
- **TypeScript** für bessere DX
- **Vitest** für Testing
- **Deployment**: Vercel/Netlify für Frontend, Railway/Fly.io für Backend

**Game-Config Flow**:
1. **Setup-Phase**: Spieler 1 konfiguriert das Spiel → braucht `configStore`

3. **Gameplay**: Config ist read-only, Teil des Spielstands

## Korrigierte State-Management-Architektur

**Pinia Stores (korrigiert)**:
- `configStore` - **Aktive Konfiguration während Setup** (YAML editing, validation)
- `connectionStore` - WebRTC-Verbindungsstatus
- `gameStore` - Temporärer UI-State (Animationen, selected pieces)

**URL-basierter State**:
```
/setup → configStore aktiv für Konfiguration
/game/abc123 → Config ist "eingefroren" in Raum-Daten, gameStore für UI
```

**Config-Lifecycle**:
1. `/setup` - `configStore` ist mutable für Konfiguration
2. "Create Game" - Config wird serialisiert und an Server gesendet
3. `/game/:roomId` - Config wird vom Server/Peer geladen (read-only)
4. Gameplay - Config ist unveränderlich Teil des Spielstands

## Implementierungsreihenfolge & Meilensteine

### Phase 1: Foundation (Woche 1-2)
**Ziel**: Grundlegende Projektstruktur und Core-Libraries integrieren

**Frontend Setup**:
1. Vite + Vue 3 + TypeScript Projekt initialisieren
2. Pinia für State-Management einrichten
3. Vue Router mit URL-basiertem State konfigurieren
4. Grundlegende UI-Komponenten (Button, Modal, etc.)

**Backend Setup**:
1. Node.js + Express Projekt initialisieren
2. WebSocket-Server für Signaling implementieren
3. Turso/SQLite Datenbankverbindung einrichten
4. Grundlegende API-Endpoints

### Phase 2: Core Game Logic (Woche 3-4)
**Ziel**: YAML-Konfigurationssystem und Spiellogik

**YAML-System**:
1. js-yaml Integration und Validierung
2. Konfigurationsschema implementieren
3. Template-System (Klassisch, Mini)
4. configStore mit Validation

**Game Engine**:
1. Spielbrett-Datenstrukturen
2. Figuren-Management und Bewegungslogik
3. Kampfsystem-Implementation
4. Spielregeln-Engine basierend auf YAML

### Phase 3: Git-basierte P2P (Woche 5-6)
**Ziel**: Innovative Git-Synchronisation implementieren

**Git Integration**:
1. isomorphic-git Setup und Repository-Management
2. Commit-per-Move Implementation
3. Fast-Forward-Merge-Logik
4. Conflict-Resolution-Mechanismus

**P2P Communication**:
1. simple-peer Integration
2. WebRTC-Verbindungsmanagement
3. Git-Commit-Übertragung via P2P
4. Reconnection-Handling

### Phase 4: User Interface (Woche 7-8)
**Ziel**: Vollständige Benutzeroberfläche

**Game Views**:
1. GameSetupView mit Konfiguration
2. GamePlayView mit interaktivem Brett
3. Drag-free Click-to-Select/Move Interface
4. Real-time Status-Updates

**Components**:
1. GameBoard mit BoardCell und GamePiece
2. PieceSelector für Setup-Phase
3. ConnectionStatus und Room-Management
4. Responsive Design für Mobile

### Phase 5: Integration & Testing (Woche 9-10)
**Ziel**: End-to-End-Funktionalität und Stabilität

**Integration**:
1. Frontend-Backend-Integration
2. Complete Game-Flow Testing
3. P2P-Synchronisation unter verschiedenen Bedingungen
4. Error-Handling und Edge-Cases

**Testing**:
1. Unit-Tests für Core-Logic
2. Integration-Tests für P2P-Flow
3. E2E-Tests für komplette Spielsessions
4. Performance-Testing und Optimierung

ToolSpecification.name: use_aws
ToolSpecification.description: Make an AWS CLI api call with the specified service, operation, and parameters. All arguments MUST conform to the AWS CLI specification. Should the output of the invocation indicate a malformed command, invoke help to obtain the the correct command.

parameters.description: The parameters for the operation. The parameter keys MUST conform to the AWS CLI specification. You should prefer to use JSON Syntax over shorthand syntax wherever possible. For parameters that are booleans, prioritize using flags with no value. Denote these flags with flag names as key and an empty string as their value. You should also prefer kebab case.

profile_name.description: Optional: AWS profile name to use from ~/.aws/credentials. Defaults to default profile if not specified.

region.description: Region name for calling the operation on AWS.

label.description: Human readable description of the api that is being called.

operation_name.description: The name of the operation to perform.

service_name.description: The name of the AWS service. If you want to query s3, you should use s3api if possible.

ToolSpecification.name: fs_read
ToolSpecification.description:
Tool for reading files (for example, `cat -n`),  directories (for example, `ls -la`) and images. If user has supplied paths that appear to be leading to images, you should use this tool right away using Image mode. The behavior of this tool is determined by the `mode` parameter. The available modes are:
- line: Show lines in a file, given by an optional `start_line` and optional `end_line`.
- directory: List directory contents. Content is returned in the "long format" of ls (that is, `ls -la`).
- search: Search for a pattern in a file. The pattern is a string. The matching is case insensitive.

Example Usage:
1. Read all lines from a file: command="line", path="/path/to/file.txt"
2. Read the last 5 lines from a file: command="line", path="/path/to/file.txt", start_line=-5
3. List the files in the home directory: command="line", path="~"
4. Recursively list files in a directory to a max depth of 2: command="line", path="/path/to/directory", depth=2
5. Search for all instances of "test" in a file: command="search", path="/path/to/file.txt", pattern="test"

context_lines.description: Number of context lines around search results (optional, for Search mode)

end_line.description: Ending line number (optional, for Line mode). A negative index represents a line number starting from the end of the file.

mode.description: The mode to run in: `Line`, `Directory`, `Search`. `Line` and `Search` are only for text files, and `Directory` is only for directories. `Image` is for image files, in this mode `image_paths` is required.

pattern.description: Pattern to search for (required, for Search mode). Case insensitive. The pattern matching is performed per line.

image_paths.description: List of paths to the images. This is currently supported by the Image mode.

depth.description: Depth of a recursive directory listing (optional, for Directory mode)

start_line.description: Starting line number (optional, for Line mode). A negative index represents a line number starting from the end of the file.

path.description: Path to the file or directory. The path should be absolute, or otherwise start with ~ for the user's home.

ToolSpecification.name: fs_write
ToolSpecification.description:
A tool for creating and editing files
 * The `create` command will override the file at `path` if it already exists as a file, and otherwise create a new file
 * The `append` command will add content to the end of an existing file, automatically adding a newline if the file doesn't end with one. The file must exist.
 Notes for using the `str_replace` command:
 * The `old_str` parameter should match EXACTLY one or more consecutive lines from the original file. Be mindful of whitespaces!
 * If the `old_str` parameter is not unique in the file, the replacement will not be performed. Make sure to include enough context in `old_str` to make it unique
 * The `new_str` parameter should contain the edited lines that should replace the `old_str`.

command.description: The commands to run. Allowed options are: `create`, `str_replace`, `insert`, `append`.

new_str.description: Required parameter of `str_replace` command containing the new string. Required parameter of `insert` command containing the string to insert. Required parameter of `append` command containing the content to append to the file.

old_str.description: Required parameter of `str_replace` command containing the string in `path` to replace.

file_text.description: Required parameter of `create` command, with the content of the file to be created.

path.description: Absolute path to file or directory, e.g. `/repo/file.py` or `/repo`.

insert_line.description: Required parameter of `insert` command. The `new_str` will be inserted AFTER the line `insert_line` of `path`.

summary.description: A brief explanation of what the file change does or why it's being made.

ToolSpecification.name: execute_bash
ToolSpecification.description: Execute the specified bash command.

summary.description: A brief explanation of what the command does

ToolSpecification.name: dummy
ToolSpecification.description: This is a dummy tool. If you are seeing this that means the tool associated with this tool call is not in the list of available tools. This could be because a wrong tool name was supplied or the list of tools has changed since the conversation has started. Do not show this when user asks you to list tools.

ToolSpecification.name: report_issue
ToolSpecification.description: Opens the browser to a pre-filled gh (GitHub) issue template to report chat issues, bugs, or feature requests. Pre-filled information includes the conversation transcript, chat context, and chat request IDs from the service.

steps_to_reproduce.description: Optional: Previous user chat requests or steps that were taken that may have resulted in the issue or error response.

expected_behavior.description: Optional: The expected chat behavior or action that did not happen.

title.description: The title of the GitHub issue.

actual_behavior.description: Optional: The actual chat behavior that happened and demonstrates the issue or lack of a feature.

ToolSpecification.name: responsible_vibe_mcp___whats_next
ToolSpecification.description: Get guidance for the current development phase and determine what to work on next. Call this tool after each user message to receive phase-specific instructions and check if you should transition to the next development phase. The tool will reference your plan file for specific tasks and context.

context.description: Brief description of what you're currently working on or discussing with the user

recent_messages.description: Recent conversation messages that provide context for the current development state

role.description: Who sent the message (user or assistant)

user_input.description: The user's most recent message or request

conversation_summary.description: Summary of the development progress and key decisions made so far

ToolSpecification.name: responsible_vibe_mcp___resume_workflow
ToolSpecification.description: Continue development after a break or conversation restart. This tool provides complete project context, current development status, and next steps to seamlessly pick up where you left off. Use when starting a new conversation about an existing project.

include_system_prompt.description: Whether to include setup instructions for the assistant (default: true)

ToolSpecification.name: responsible_vibe_mcp___get_tool_info
ToolSpecification.description: Get comprehensive information about the responsible-vibe-mcp development workflow tools for better tool discoverability and AI integration. Returns detailed information about all available tools, workflows, core concepts, and usage guidelines.

ToolSpecification.name: responsible_vibe_mcp___start_development
ToolSpecification.description: Begin a new development project with a structured workflow. Choose from different development approaches (waterfall, bugfix, epcc) or use a custom workflow. This tool sets up the project plan and initializes the development process.

workflow.description:
Choose your development workflow:

• **bugfix**: bugfix - A focused workflow for bug fixing: Reproduce, Analyze, Fix, Verify - optimized for debugging and fixing existing issues
• **epcc**: epcc - A comprehensive development workflow based on Anthropic's best practices: Explore, Plan, Code, Commit - ideal for smaller features and iterative development
• **greenfield**: greenfield - A comprehensive workflow for starting new projects from scratch: Ideation, Architecture, Plan, Code, Document - ideal for greenfield projects requiring thorough upfront planning
• **minor**: minor - A streamlined workflow for small-impact changes: Explore (analysis + design) → Implement (code + test + commit) - optimized for minor enhancements
• **posts**: posts - A comprehensive workflow for writing posts - from blog posts to short-form content, with research, story development, and multi-platform publishing
• **slides**: slides - A comprehensive workflow for creating presentations - tool-agnostic approach that works with Slidev, PowerPoint, Google Slides, or any presentation platform
• **waterfall**: waterfall - From Specification down to test – the historical way. Ideal for larger, design-heavy tasks with well-defined requirements
• **custom**: Use custom workflow from .vibe/workflow.yaml in your project

Default: waterfall (recommended for larger, design-heavy tasks)

require_reviews.description: Whether to require reviews before phase transitions. When enabled, use conduct_review tool before proceeding to next phase.

commit_behaviour.description: Git commit behavior: Use "none" as this is not a git repository. Other options ("step", "phase", "end") are not applicable for non-git projects.

ToolSpecification.name: responsible_vibe_mcp___reset_development
ToolSpecification.description: Start over with a clean slate by deleting all development progress and conversation history. This permanently removes the project plan and resets the development state. Use when you want to completely restart the development approach for a project.

confirm.description: Must be true to execute reset - prevents accidental resets

reason.description: Optional reason for reset (for logging and audit trail)

ToolSpecification.name: responsible_vibe_mcp___conduct_review
ToolSpecification.description: Conduct a review of the current phase before proceeding to the next phase. This tool analyzes artifacts and decisions from the current phase using defined review perspectives. Use this tool when reviews are required before phase transitions.

target_phase.description: The target phase you want to transition to after the review is complete

ToolSpecification.name: responsible_vibe_mcp___list_workflows
ToolSpecification.description: Get an overview of all available workflows with their descriptions and resource URIs. Use this to understand what development workflows are available and access detailed workflow information through the provided resource URIs.

ToolSpecification.name: responsible_vibe_mcp___proceed_to_phase
ToolSpecification.description: Move to a specific development phase when the current phase is complete. Use this tool to explicitly transition between phases. Check your plan file to see available phases for the current workflow. Only transition when current phase tasks are finished and user confirms readiness.

reason.description: Why you're moving to this phase now (e.g., "requirements complete", "user approved design", "implementation finished")

target_phase.description: The development phase to move to. Check your plan file section headers to see available phases for the current workflow

review_state.description: Review state for transitions that require reviews. Use "not-required" when reviews are disabled, "pending" when review is needed, "performed" when review is complete.

fullPage.description: When true, takes a screenshot of the full scrollable page, instead of the currently visible viewport. Cannot be used with element screenshots.

filename.description: File name to save the screenshot to. Defaults to `page-{timestamp}.{png|jpeg}` if not specified.

raw.description: Whether to return without compression (in PNG format). Default is false, which returns a JPEG image.

ref.description: Exact target element reference from the page snapshot. If not provided, the screenshot will be taken of viewport. If ref is provided, element must be provided too.

element.description: Human-readable element description used to obtain permission to screenshot the element. If not provided, the screenshot will be taken of viewport. If element is provided, ref must be provided too.
